# Field Interfaces as Semantic Substrates: A General Model for Relational UI State

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 09 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader theory paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 09 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 01, Paper 02, Paper 03, Paper 04, Paper 05, Paper 06, Paper 07, Paper 08  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

A field interface is an interface whose actionable state is not only local component state but a shared semantic substrate: participants emit, receive, preserve, and expose relational signals inside a common coordinate and meaning space. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — component state, scene graphs, reactive views, retained/immediate UI, and information visualization — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: a field interface is an interface whose actionable state is not only local component state but a shared semantic substrate: participants emit, receive, preserve, and expose relational signals inside a common coordinate and meaning space.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. A system becomes interesting when relationships, evidence, attention, memory, diagnostics, accessibility equivalents, and data lifecycle stop being scattered across component props and one-off event handlers and become inspectable participants in a shared model.

This distinction matters because many expressive interfaces fail in the same pattern. They use motion, canvas, color, badges, animation, or layout to suggest a relation, but the relation itself is not available to the system. It cannot be inspected, tested, degraded for accessibility, serialized as a recipe, or bound to data in another view. Meaning appears at the surface but is not carried as a durable substrate. Fundamental's vocabulary is one answer to that failure, but the broader research question is whether interface systems should treat such meaning as a first-class layer.

---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [fundamental-site]; [fundamental-engine-repo]; [retained-immediate-ui]; [react-docs-declarative]; [bostock2011]; [ragan2016]; [abdul2018].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is theoretical validation, not missing bibliography: the paper proposes a unifying model that should be judged by whether it usefully explains the eight existing papers and yields testable predictions in later domain studies.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper is grounded in three source groups. Fundamental's public documentation and repository anchors support the system-specific claim that the project is a renderer-agnostic relational field engine rather than a single DOM decoration layer [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]. Retained/immediate-mode UI documentation, React's component-tree framing, and D3's data-driven document model provide the comparison set for conventional interface-state and rendering substrates [retained-immediate-ui; react-docs-declarative; bostock2011]. Provenance and intelligible/explainable-systems literature supports the narrower claim that inspectability depends on making the basis of a system's behavior available for review, not merely rendering an effect [ragan2016; abdul2018].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Accessibility standards treat semantic availability and operability as non-negotiable constraints. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

## 3. Core model

The paper's model can be stated in four terms.

**Participant.** A participant is anything that can emit, receive, store, transform, or explain interface state. Participants include DOM elements, data records, relationships, layout regions, user attention, events, diagnostics, and visual layers. A rendered object is a participant only if it can be addressed by the model; visibility alone is not participation.

**Signal.** A signal is a typed property that can move between participants or be read from them. Examples include attention, memory, confidence, contradiction, priority, risk, uncertainty, relationship strength, and diagnostic cause. Signals may be visualized, but they should not be confused with the visualization.

**Binding.** A binding connects a participant to a source of meaning. A data record binds to a body; a source binds to a claim; a citation binds to an evidence relation; a motion trail binds to a semantic state; a diagnostic view binds to the runtime state it reveals. The binding determines whether the representation can be checked.

**Equivalence.** An equivalence is the rule by which a signal survives when a channel changes. Motion can become static emphasis; color can become text or shape; a canvas rendering can become semantic DOM; a particle path can become a relationship label. Equivalence is what prevents expressive interfaces from becoming inaccessible when their preferred representation is unavailable.

The model is falsifiable. If an interface shows a relation but the relation cannot be queried, it is not a participant. If a signal appears visually but has no semantic source, it is a decoration, not semantic state. If a reduced or alternate rendering drops meaning that the primary rendering carried, equivalence has failed. If diagnostics mutate the state they claim to explain, the diagnostic claim is invalid.

---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Define semantic substrate as the layer that carries relationships, memory, attention, uncertainty, evidence, diagnostics, and accessibility equivalents.
- Position field interfaces against component state, scene graphs, reactive rendering, and visualization layers.
- State the five invariants of the model: participation, reciprocity, conservation (in the engine's sense — particle *count* is the one strong invariant; energy and momentum are intentionally not conserved, per the caveat canon), semantic anchoring, and inspectability.
- Show how the eight earlier papers instantiate one general substrate rather than eight disconnected systems.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

---

## 5. Design implications

The first implication is that interface behavior should be authored against explicit relations rather than against visual side effects. A designer should be able to name a support relation, a contradiction, a memory trace, an attention budget, or a priority field without smuggling that concept into an animation curve or color rule. This is the reason the recipe model matters: it separates product-language concepts from executable runtime tokens.

The second implication is that accessibility should be evaluated at the level of meaning. Turning off motion is not enough if motion was the only carrier of origin, direction, or causality. Removing color is not enough if color was the only carrier of confidence or risk. Replacing a canvas with fallback text is not enough if the relationships encoded by the canvas disappear. The substrate has to preserve the source meaning and expose an alternate carrier.

The third implication is that diagnostics should be part of the interface model rather than an afterthought. If the interface can compute a behavior, it should be able to expose why that behavior occurred. A runtime-inspectable interface does not require every user to inspect it, but it gives authors, auditors, and advanced users a path to check the system's claims.

The fourth implication is that data binding should preserve relational meaning. Rendering an array of records into cards loses the graph unless links, confidence, uncertainty, and lifecycle are also bound. A relational interface should not flatten data and then rebuild its meaning with badges.

---

## 6. Evaluation plan

A mature version of this paper would be evaluated through a family of studies rather than one omnibus experiment.

The first study would compare a conventional interface against a substrate-aware version on tasks that require orientation, refinding, and relationship awareness. The expected outcomes would include time to recover context, accuracy of relation identification, and subjective orientation.

The second study would test trust calibration. Participants would answer source-backed questions using either citations and badges or an explicit evidence graph. Outcomes would include discrimination between supported and unsupported claims, calibration error, and over-reliance under time pressure.

The third study would test semantic degradation. Participants would use a full expressive surface and a degraded surface with motion, color, or canvas effects removed. A non-inferiority design would ask whether comprehension and orientation are preserved when the expressive channel disappears.

The fourth study would test authoring and debugging. Authors would diagnose why a behavior occurred with and without runtime diagnostics. Outcomes would include time-to-diagnose, fix accuracy, and perceived confidence.

The studies should be preregistered and analyzed with mixed-effects models where tasks and participants are crossed, following the methods pattern used in the first family [baayen2008; nosek2018].

---

## 7. Implementation status and limitations

This draft should not be read as a claim that all proposed behavior is currently shipped. The first family already distinguishes shipped runtime features, study-page demonstrations, proposed harnesses, and unrun evaluations. The same discipline applies here. Fundamental provides a concrete implementation vocabulary — bodies, fields, recipes, registries, diagnostics, data binding, host boundaries — but this expansion paper argues the broader model.

The main limitation is abstraction risk. A general paper can become too broad to falsify. To avoid that, every concept above is tied to a failure condition: unqueryable relations, unsupported signals, inaccessible channel loss, mutating diagnostics, and flattened data. Future revisions should preserve those failure tests.

A second limitation is empirical status. The external bibliography now supports the paper's background and evaluation frame, but the paper's central proposal still requires the study or implementation evidence named in the research gap.

---

## 8. Conclusion

A field interface is an interface whose actionable state is not only local component state but a shared semantic substrate: participants emit, receive, preserve, and expose relational signals inside a common coordinate and meaning space. The claim is broader than Fundamental but made concrete by it. The first paper family shows the pieces: reading memory, evidence graphs, reduced-motion equivalence, host-driven runtime structure, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This expansion paper turns those pieces into a general research question: what would interface systems look like if relational meaning were treated as a substrate rather than an ornament?

========================================================================
FILE: docs/research/10-conserved-attention-interface-systems.md
========================================================================

# Conserved Attention in Interface Systems

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 10 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader hci paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 10 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 01, Paper 02, Paper 03, Paper 08  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Attention in complex interfaces should be treated as a finite, transferable resource: emphasis gained by one region must be paid for by less emphasis elsewhere, and systems should expose that budget rather than multiplying signals without cost. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — attention, salience, interruption, cognitive load, and notification design — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: attention in complex interfaces should be treated as a finite, transferable resource: emphasis gained by one region must be paid for by less emphasis elsewhere, and systems should expose that budget rather than multiplying signals without cost.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. A system becomes interesting when relationships, evidence, attention, memory, diagnostics, accessibility equivalents, and data lifecycle stop being scattered across component props and one-off event handlers and become inspectable participants in a shared model.

This distinction matters because many expressive interfaces fail in the same pattern. They use motion, canvas, color, badges, animation, or layout to suggest a relation, but the relation itself is not available to the system. It cannot be inspected, tested, degraded for accessibility, serialized as a recipe, or bound to data in another view. Meaning appears at the surface but is not carried as a durable substrate. Fundamental's vocabulary is one answer to that failure, but the broader research question is whether interface systems should treat such meaning as a first-class layer.

---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [fundamental-site]; [itti1998]; [bailey2006]; [mark2008]; [hart1988]; [hornbaek2006].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is empirical: the paper needs a controlled study showing whether budgeted emphasis improves orientation, decision quality, or interruption recovery compared with unconstrained highlighting.

## 2. Background and related work

The attention-budget claim is supported by a deliberately narrow source set. Computational-salience work provides the contrast case: salience predicts where the eye may be drawn, while this paper proposes an explicit interface-level budget over semantic emphasis [itti1998]. Interruption-cost and workload research support the claim that attention shifts are measurable and costly rather than free interface decoration [bailey2006; mark2008; hart1988]. Usability-measurement work supports the evaluation framing: conserved attention should be tested through task performance, error, workload, and user experience rather than asserted from visual appeal [hornbaek2006]. Fundamental's public examples give the concrete system case for a fixed attention budget in which emphasizing one item reduces emphasis elsewhere [fundamental-site].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Accessibility standards treat semantic availability and operability as non-negotiable constraints. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

## 3. Core model

The paper's model can be stated in four terms.

**Participant.** A participant is anything that can emit, receive, store, transform, or explain interface state. Participants include DOM elements, data records, relationships, layout regions, user attention, events, diagnostics, and visual layers. A rendered object is a participant only if it can be addressed by the model; visibility alone is not participation.

**Signal.** A signal is a typed property that can move between participants or be read from them. Examples include attention, memory, confidence, contradiction, priority, risk, uncertainty, relationship strength, and diagnostic cause. Signals may be visualized, but they should not be confused with the visualization.

**Binding.** A binding connects a participant to a source of meaning. A data record binds to a body; a source binds to a claim; a citation binds to an evidence relation; a motion trail binds to a semantic state; a diagnostic view binds to the runtime state it reveals. The binding determines whether the representation can be checked.

**Equivalence.** An equivalence is the rule by which a signal survives when a channel changes. Motion can become static emphasis; color can become text or shape; a canvas rendering can become semantic DOM; a particle path can become a relationship label. Equivalence is what prevents expressive interfaces from becoming inaccessible when their preferred representation is unavailable.

The model is falsifiable. If an interface shows a relation but the relation cannot be queried, it is not a participant. If a signal appears visually but has no semantic source, it is a decoration, not semantic state. If a reduced or alternate rendering drops meaning that the primary rendering carried, equivalence has failed. If diagnostics mutate the state they claim to explain, the diagnostic claim is invalid.

---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Define conserved attention as a budgeted field over interface participants.
- Separate attention from selection, focus, visibility, and notification.
- Show how documents, AI evidence, dashboards, and diagnostics consume or conserve attention.
- Propose measures for attention transfer, attention debt, and interruption cost.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

---

## 5. Design implications

The first implication is that interface behavior should be authored against explicit relations rather than against visual side effects. A designer should be able to name a support relation, a contradiction, a memory trace, an attention budget, or a priority field without smuggling that concept into an animation curve or color rule. This is the reason the recipe model matters: it separates product-language concepts from executable runtime tokens.

The second implication is that accessibility should be evaluated at the level of meaning. Turning off motion is not enough if motion was the only carrier of origin, direction, or causality. Removing color is not enough if color was the only carrier of confidence or risk. Replacing a canvas with fallback text is not enough if the relationships encoded by the canvas disappear. The substrate has to preserve the source meaning and expose an alternate carrier.

The third implication is that diagnostics should be part of the interface model rather than an afterthought. If the interface can compute a behavior, it should be able to expose why that behavior occurred. A runtime-inspectable interface does not require every user to inspect it, but it gives authors, auditors, and advanced users a path to check the system's claims.

The fourth implication is that data binding should preserve relational meaning. Rendering an array of records into cards loses the graph unless links, confidence, uncertainty, and lifecycle are also bound. A relational interface should not flatten data and then rebuild its meaning with badges.

---

## 6. Evaluation plan

A mature version of this paper would be evaluated through a family of studies rather than one omnibus experiment.

The first study would compare a conventional interface against a substrate-aware version on tasks that require orientation, refinding, and relationship awareness. The expected outcomes would include time to recover context, accuracy of relation identification, and subjective orientation.

The second study would test trust calibration. Participants would answer source-backed questions using either citations and badges or an explicit evidence graph. Outcomes would include discrimination between supported and unsupported claims, calibration error, and over-reliance under time pressure.

The third study would test semantic degradation. Participants would use a full expressive surface and a degraded surface with motion, color, or canvas effects removed. A non-inferiority design would ask whether comprehension and orientation are preserved when the expressive channel disappears.

The fourth study would test authoring and debugging. Authors would diagnose why a behavior occurred with and without runtime diagnostics. Outcomes would include time-to-diagnose, fix accuracy, and perceived confidence.

The studies should be preregistered and analyzed with mixed-effects models where tasks and participants are crossed, following the methods pattern used in the first family [baayen2008; nosek2018].

---

## 7. Implementation status and limitations

This draft should not be read as a claim that all proposed behavior is currently shipped. The first family already distinguishes shipped runtime features, study-page demonstrations, proposed harnesses, and unrun evaluations. The same discipline applies here. Fundamental provides a concrete implementation vocabulary — bodies, fields, recipes, registries, diagnostics, data binding, host boundaries — but this expansion paper argues the broader model.

The main limitation is abstraction risk. A general paper can become too broad to falsify. To avoid that, every concept above is tied to a failure condition: unqueryable relations, unsupported signals, inaccessible channel loss, mutating diagnostics, and flattened data. Future revisions should preserve those failure tests.

A second limitation is empirical status. The external bibliography now supports the paper's background and evaluation frame, but the paper's central proposal still requires the study or implementation evidence named in the research gap.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Attention in complex interfaces should be treated as a finite, transferable resource: emphasis gained by one region must be paid for by less emphasis elsewhere, and systems should expose that budget rather than multiplying signals without cost. The claim is broader than Fundamental but made concrete by it. The first paper family shows the pieces: reading memory, evidence graphs, reduced-motion equivalence, host-driven runtime structure, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This expansion paper turns those pieces into a general research question: what would interface systems look like if relational meaning were treated as a substrate rather than an ornament?

========================================================================
FILE: docs/research/11-semantic-degradation-paths.md
========================================================================

# Semantic Degradation Paths for Accessible Interactive Systems

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 11 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader accessibility paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 11 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 04, Paper 16, Paper 22, Paper 25  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Every expressive channel in an interface should have a semantic degradation path: when motion, color, spatial effects, particles, glow, or high-fidelity rendering is removed, the meaning carried by that channel must remain available through another representation. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — accessibility, channel loss, reduced motion, use of color, and semantic fallback — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: every expressive channel in an interface should have a semantic degradation path: when motion, color, spatial effects, particles, glow, or high-fidelity rendering is removed, the meaning carried by that channel must remain available through another representation.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. A system becomes interesting when relationships, evidence, attention, memory, diagnostics, accessibility equivalents, and data lifecycle stop being scattered across component props and one-off event handlers and become inspectable participants in a shared model.

They use motion, canvas, color, badges, animation, or layout to suggest a relation, but the relation itself is not available to the system. ---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [wcag-motion]; [use-of-color]; [prefers-reduced-motion]; [vestibular-accessibility]; [piaggio2012].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is to test whether generalized degradation paths preserve meaning across channels beyond motion and color, especially for glow, spatial layout, particle fields, and canvas/WebGL surfaces.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper's normative claims are standards-led. WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 2.3.3 supports the premise that animation triggered by interaction must be controllable, and Media Queries Level 5 provides the platform signal for reduced-motion preference [wcag-motion; prefers-reduced-motion]. WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 1.4.1 supports the broader redundancy principle that an expressive channel such as color must not be the only carrier of information [use-of-color]. WAI motion-related guidance supports the more cautious accessibility claim that motion can be a barrier for some users and should not be required to recover meaning [vestibular-accessibility]. Non-inferiority reporting guidance is used only for the proposed evaluation design, not as evidence that the proposed degradation model already works [piaggio2012].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Accessibility standards treat semantic availability and operability as non-negotiable constraints. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Generalize reduced-motion equivalence into channel-equivalence.
- Define a degradation graph from source meaning to expressive carriers and accessible equivalents.
- Distinguish decorative removal from semantic preservation.
- Propose conformance gates for channel-redundancy, source trace, and loss testing.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

# Evidence Graphs as User Interfaces for AI Trust Calibration

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 12 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader ai-hci paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 12 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 03, Paper 19, Paper 29  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Source-backed AI interfaces should expose evidence as a graph of claim-level support, contradiction, uncertainty, and provenance rather than as inline citation ornaments and scalar confidence badges. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — AI trust, evidence graphs, citation faithfulness, automation bias, and confidence calibration — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: source-backed ai interfaces should expose evidence as a graph of claim-level support, contradiction, uncertainty, and provenance rather than as inline citation ornaments and scalar confidence badges.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. They use motion, canvas, color, badges, animation, or layout to suggest a relation, but the relation itself is not available to the system. ---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [lee2004]; [parasuraman1997]; [skitka1999]; [goddard2012]; [liu2023verifiability]; [gao2023alce]; [wallat2024faithfulness]; [bucinca2021]; [kim2025llm]; [signal-detection]; [brier1950].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is empirical: the paper needs a user study comparing graph-based support displays with inline citations and confidence badges on calibrated reliance, source inspection, and contradiction detection.

## 2. Background and related work

The trust claim rests on established human-automation and recent LLM-attribution work. Trust calibration literature defines the target as appropriate reliance rather than maximal trust [lee2004], while automation-bias research explains why fluent system output can be overused or misused under decision pressure [parasuraman1997; skitka1999; goddard2012]. Recent generative-search and RAG-attribution evaluations show that citations and generated claims can fail at the level this paper cares about: source attachment is not the same as sentence support, entailment, or faithful attribution [liu2023verifiability; gao2023alce; wallat2024faithfulness]. Work on cognitive forcing and LLM reliance supports the caution that explanations, sources, and inconsistency cues can change reliance behavior, sometimes in different directions for correct and incorrect answers [bucinca2021; kim2025llm]. Signal-detection and probabilistic-calibration sources define the proposed evaluation measures [signal-detection; brier1950].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Accessibility standards treat semantic availability and operability as non-negotiable constraints. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

## 3. Core model

The paper's model can be stated in four terms.

**Participant.** A participant is anything that can emit, receive, store, transform, or explain interface state. Participants include DOM elements, data records, relationships, layout regions, user attention, events, diagnostics, and visual layers. A rendered object is a participant only if it can be addressed by the model; visibility alone is not participation.

**Signal.** A signal is a typed property that can move between participants or be read from them. Examples include attention, memory, confidence, contradiction, priority, risk, uncertainty, relationship strength, and diagnostic cause. Signals may be visualized, but they should not be confused with the visualization.

**Binding.** A binding connects a participant to a source of meaning. A data record binds to a body; a source binds to a claim; a citation binds to an evidence relation; a motion trail binds to a semantic state; a diagnostic view binds to the runtime state it reveals. The binding determines whether the representation can be checked.

**Equivalence.** An equivalence is the rule by which a signal survives when a channel changes. Motion can become static emphasis; color can become text or shape; a canvas rendering can become semantic DOM; a particle path can become a relationship label. Equivalence is what prevents expressive interfaces from becoming inaccessible when their preferred representation is unavailable.

The model is falsifiable. If an interface shows a relation but the relation cannot be queried, it is not a participant. If a signal appears visually but has no semantic source, it is a decoration, not semantic state. If a reduced or alternate rendering drops meaning that the primary rendering carried, equivalence has failed. If diagnostics mutate the state they claim to explain, the diagnostic claim is invalid.

---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Define claim-source graphs as the unit of trust interaction.
- Separate source attachment from source support, contradiction, entailment, and provenance.
- Compare badges, citation markers, provenance panels, and graph surfaces.
- Propose calibration outcomes using signal detection and probabilistic calibration.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

---

To avoid that, every concept above is tied to a failure condition: unqueryable relations, unsupported signals, inaccessible channel loss, mutating diagnostics, and flattened data. Future revisions should preserve those failure tests.

A second limitation is empirical status. The external bibliography now supports the paper's background and evaluation frame, but the paper's central proposal still requires the study or implementation evidence named in the research gap.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Source-backed AI interfaces should expose evidence as a graph of claim-level support, contradiction, uncertainty, and provenance rather than as inline citation ornaments and scalar confidence badges. The claim is broader than Fundamental but made concrete by it. The first paper family shows the pieces: reading memory, evidence graphs, reduced-motion equivalence, host-driven runtime structure, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This expansion paper turns those pieces into a general research question: what would interface systems look like if relational meaning were treated as a substrate rather than an ornament?

========================================================================
FILE: docs/research/13-runtime-inspectable-interfaces.md
========================================================================

# Runtime-Inspectable Interfaces: Diagnostics as a First-Class UX Primitive

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 13 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader diagnostics paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 13 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 08, Paper 20  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Interactive systems should expose inspectable explanations of their own behavior: not only source-level debugging for developers, but runtime-level answers to why an element changed, which relation acted, and what state caused the response. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — diagnostics, devtools, explainability, observability, and end-user transparency — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: interactive systems should expose inspectable explanations of their own behavior: not only source-level debugging for developers, but runtime-level answers to why an element changed, which relation acted, and what state caused the response.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. A system becomes interesting when relationships, evidence, attention, memory, diagnostics, accessibility equivalents, and data lifecycle stop being scattered across component props and one-off event handlers and become inspectable participants in a shared model.

This distinction matters because many expressive interfaces fail in the same pattern. They use motion, canvas, color, badges, animation, or layout to suggest a relation, but the relation itself is not available to the system. It cannot be inspected, tested, degraded for accessibility, serialized as a recipe, or bound to data in another view. Meaning appears at the surface but is not carried as a durable substrate. Fundamental's vocabulary is one answer to that failure, but the broader research question is whether interface systems should treat such meaning as a first-class layer.

---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [chrome-devtools]; [firefox-devtools]; [lim2009]; [abdul2018]; [ragan2016]; [kulesza2013].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is to measure whether runtime-inspectable interfaces reduce diagnosis time, author error, or misplaced trust compared with conventional devtools and documentation.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper's source package separates developer inspection from user-facing intelligibility. Browser DevTools documentation anchors the practical lineage of live, read-only inspection surfaces for running interfaces [chrome-devtools; firefox-devtools]. Intelligibility and explainable-systems work supports the claim that systems are more accountable when users or authors can ask why, why not, what if, and how behavior occurred [lim2009; abdul2018]. Provenance and explanatory-debugging work supports the narrower design requirement that explanations should expose the basis of a behavior and help authors correct or calibrate the system rather than merely display a static label [ragan2016; kulesza2013].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Accessibility standards treat semantic availability and operability as non-negotiable constraints. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

## 3. Core model

The paper's model can be stated in four terms.

**Participant.** A participant is anything that can emit, receive, store, transform, or explain interface state. Participants include DOM elements, data records, relationships, layout regions, user attention, events, diagnostics, and visual layers. A rendered object is a participant only if it can be addressed by the model; visibility alone is not participation.

**Signal.** A signal is a typed property that can move between participants or be read from them. Examples include attention, memory, confidence, contradiction, priority, risk, uncertainty, relationship strength, and diagnostic cause. Signals may be visualized, but they should not be confused with the visualization.

**Binding.** A binding connects a participant to a source of meaning. A data record binds to a body; a source binds to a claim; a citation binds to an evidence relation; a motion trail binds to a semantic state; a diagnostic view binds to the runtime state it reveals. The binding determines whether the representation can be checked.

**Equivalence.** An equivalence is the rule by which a signal survives when a channel changes. Motion can become static emphasis; color can become text or shape; a canvas rendering can become semantic DOM; a particle path can become a relationship label. Equivalence is what prevents expressive interfaces from becoming inaccessible when their preferred representation is unavailable.

The model is falsifiable. If an interface shows a relation but the relation cannot be queried, it is not a participant. If a signal appears visually but has no semantic source, it is a decoration, not semantic state. If a reduced or alternate rendering drops meaning that the primary rendering carried, equivalence has failed. If diagnostics mutate the state they claim to explain, the diagnostic claim is invalid.

---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Define runtime inspectability as a UX primitive distinct from source debugging.
- Classify diagnostic questions: cause, relation, state, cost, prediction, provenance.
- Explain the reveal-never-mutate discipline.
- Position field diagnostics against browser devtools and explainable systems.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

---

## 5. Design implications

The first implication is that interface behavior should be authored against explicit relations rather than against visual side effects. A designer should be able to name a support relation, a contradiction, a memory trace, an attention budget, or a priority field without smuggling that concept into an animation curve or color rule. This is the reason the recipe model matters: it separates product-language concepts from executable runtime tokens.

The second implication is that accessibility should be evaluated at the level of meaning. Turning off motion is not enough if motion was the only carrier of origin, direction, or causality. Removing color is not enough if color was the only carrier of confidence or risk. Replacing a canvas with fallback text is not enough if the relationships encoded by the canvas disappear. The substrate has to preserve the source meaning and expose an alternate carrier.

The third implication is that diagnostics should be part of the interface model rather than an afterthought. If the interface can compute a behavior, it should be able to expose why that behavior occurred. A runtime-inspectable interface does not require every user to inspect it, but it gives authors, auditors, and advanced users a path to check the system's claims.

The fourth implication is that data binding should preserve relational meaning. Rendering an array of records into cards loses the graph unless links, confidence, uncertainty, and lifecycle are also bound. A relational interface should not flatten data and then rebuild its meaning with badges.

---

## 6. Evaluation plan

A mature version of this paper would be evaluated through a family of studies rather than one omnibus experiment.

The first study would compare a conventional interface against a substrate-aware version on tasks that require orientation, refinding, and relationship awareness. The expected outcomes would include time to recover context, accuracy of relation identification, and subjective orientation.

The second study would test trust calibration. Participants would answer source-backed questions using either citations and badges or an explicit evidence graph. Outcomes would include discrimination between supported and unsupported claims, calibration error, and over-reliance under time pressure.

The third study would test semantic degradation. Participants would use a full expressive surface and a degraded surface with motion, color, or canvas effects removed. A non-inferiority design would ask whether comprehension and orientation are preserved when the expressive channel disappears.

The fourth study would test authoring and debugging. Authors would diagnose why a behavior occurred with and without runtime diagnostics. Outcomes would include time-to-diagnose, fix accuracy, and perceived confidence.

The studies should be preregistered and analyzed with mixed-effects models where tasks and participants are crossed, following the methods pattern used in the first family [baayen2008; nosek2018].

---

## 7. Implementation status and limitations

This draft should not be read as a claim that all proposed behavior is currently shipped. The first family already distinguishes shipped runtime features, study-page demonstrations, proposed harnesses, and unrun evaluations. The same discipline applies here. Fundamental provides a concrete implementation vocabulary — bodies, fields, recipes, registries, diagnostics, data binding, host boundaries — but this expansion paper argues the broader model.

The main limitation is abstraction risk. A general paper can become too broad to falsify. To avoid that, every concept above is tied to a failure condition: unqueryable relations, unsupported signals, inaccessible channel loss, mutating diagnostics, and flattened data. Future revisions should preserve those failure tests.

A second limitation is empirical status. The external bibliography now supports the paper's background and evaluation frame, but the paper's central proposal still requires the study or implementation evidence named in the research gap.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Interactive systems should expose inspectable explanations of their own behavior: not only source-level debugging for developers, but runtime-level answers to why an element changed, which relation acted, and what state caused the response. The claim is broader than Fundamental but made concrete by it. The first paper family shows the pieces: reading memory, evidence graphs, reduced-motion equivalence, host-driven runtime structure, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This expansion paper turns those pieces into a general research question: what would interface systems look like if relational meaning were treated as a substrate rather than an ornament?

========================================================================
FILE: docs/research/14-recipes-behavioral-design-tokens.md
========================================================================

# Recipes as Behavioral Design Tokens

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 14 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader design-systems paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 14 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 06, Paper 17, Paper 22  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Design systems need portable tokens for behavior, not only visual values: named, validated, inspectable recipes can make motion, attention, evidence, priority, and relationship behavior reusable without turning prose into hidden imperative code. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — design tokens, behavior tokens, recipes, design systems, and authoring — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: design systems need portable tokens for behavior, not only visual values: named, validated, inspectable recipes can make motion, attention, evidence, priority, and relationship behavior reusable without turning prose into hidden imperative code.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. Fundamental's vocabulary is one answer to that failure, but the broader research question is whether interface systems should treat such meaning as a first-class layer.

---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [w3c-design-tokens]; [fowler2010dsl]; [resnick2009scratch]; [myers1990garnet]; [fundamental-engine-recipes].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is adoption and authoring evidence: whether designers and engineers can use behavioral tokens/recipes more reliably than bespoke interaction code.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper uses design-token work as an analogy, not as a claim of equivalence. Design Tokens Format work supports the premise that portable named values can stabilize visual decisions across systems [w3c-design-tokens]. DSL and end-user/visual programming literature supports the broader authoring claim that constrained, typed vocabularies can make complex behavior more reusable and reviewable than one-off imperative code [fowler2010dsl; resnick2009scratch; myers1990garnet]. Fundamental's recipe documentation supplies the system-specific case: recipes are behavior records whose concepts, runtime tokens, metrics, diagnostics, conditions, and accessibility equivalents are separated into auditable lanes [fundamental-engine-recipes].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. ---

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Map visual-token properties to behavioral-token properties.
- Define a behavioral token as a named, versioned, validated behavior contract.
- Show why behavior tokens need diagnostics, reduced-mode equivalents, and runtime symbol tables.
- Position recipes as data, not code.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

# Data Binding Beyond Views: From Reactive Rendering to Relational Participation

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 15 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader systems-hci paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 15 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 07, Paper 23, Paper 28  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Data binding should not only keep views synchronized with records; it should preserve the relationships, uncertainty, priority, lifecycle, and provenance that made the records meaningful before they were rendered. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — data binding, reactive UI, D3 joins, relationships, and lifecycle — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. ## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [gossman2005]; [react-docs-declarative]; [bostock2011]; [d3-selection-join]; [react-window].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is to evaluate whether relational bindings improve user outcomes over conventional reactive views for tasks involving uncertainty, dependency, provenance, or lifecycle state.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper's comparison is between view reactivity and relational participation. MVVM and React documentation support the baseline model in which application state is bound or rendered into view structure [gossman2005; react-docs-declarative]. D3 and its data-join model support the data-driven-documents lineage in which records are bound to visual/DOM marks and maintained across enter, update, and exit lifecycles [bostock2011; d3-selection-join]. List-virtualization documentation is relevant only to the performance boundary: rendering many items efficiently is different from preserving the relationships, uncertainty, priority, and lifecycle semantics those items carry [react-window].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. ---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Contrast view synchronization with relational participation.
- Define the record-to-body, link-to-edge, and state-to-metric mappings.
- Explain lifecycle as semantic transition: enter, update, exit, decay.
- Propose evaluation against flat reactive baselines.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.
