Outcomes would include discrimination between supported and unsupported claims, calibration error, and over-reliance under time pressure.

The third study would test semantic degradation. Participants would use a full expressive surface and a degraded surface with motion, color, or canvas effects removed. A non-inferiority design would ask whether comprehension and orientation are preserved when the expressive channel disappears.

The fourth study would test authoring and debugging. Authors would diagnose why a behavior occurred with and without runtime diagnostics. Outcomes would include time-to-diagnose, fix accuracy, and perceived confidence.

The studies should be preregistered and analyzed with mixed-effects models where tasks and participants are crossed, following the methods pattern used in the first family [baayen2008; nosek2018].

---

## 7. Implementation status and limitations

This draft should not be read as a claim that all proposed behavior is currently shipped. The first family already distinguishes shipped runtime features, study-page demonstrations, proposed harnesses, and unrun evaluations. The same discipline applies here. Fundamental provides a concrete implementation vocabulary — bodies, fields, recipes, registries, diagnostics, data binding, host boundaries — but this expansion paper argues the broader model.

The main limitation is abstraction risk. A general paper can become too broad to falsify. To avoid that, every concept above is tied to a failure condition: unqueryable relations, unsupported signals, inaccessible channel loss, mutating diagnostics, and flattened data. Future revisions should preserve those failure tests.

A second limitation is empirical status. The external bibliography now supports the paper's background and evaluation frame, but the paper's central proposal still requires the study or implementation evidence named in the research gap.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Data binding should not only keep views synchronized with records; it should preserve the relationships, uncertainty, priority, lifecycle, and provenance that made the records meaningful before they were rendered. The claim is broader than Fundamental but made concrete by it. The first paper family shows the pieces: reading memory, evidence graphs, reduced-motion equivalence, host-driven runtime structure, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This expansion paper turns those pieces into a general research question: what would interface systems look like if relational meaning were treated as a substrate rather than an ornament?

========================================================================
FILE: docs/research/16-semantic-canvas-problem.md
========================================================================

# The Semantic Canvas Problem

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 16 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader web-platform paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 16 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 04, Paper 11, Paper 30  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Canvas, WebGL, and high-fidelity visual layers often sever expressive behavior from semantic structure; platform-native expressive systems should keep semantic HTML canonical and bind advanced visuals to it as secondary representations. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — canvas, WebGL, semantic HTML, accessibility tree, and visual binding — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: canvas, webgl, and high-fidelity visual layers often sever expressive behavior from semantic structure; platform-native expressive systems should keep semantic html canonical and bind advanced visuals to it as secondary representations.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. A system becomes interesting when relationships, evidence, attention, memory, diagnostics, accessibility equivalents, and data lifecycle stop being scattered across component props and one-off event handlers and become inspectable participants in a shared model.

This distinction matters because many expressive interfaces fail in the same pattern. They use motion, canvas, color, badges, animation, or layout to suggest a relation, but the relation itself is not available to the system. It cannot be inspected, tested, degraded for accessibility, serialized as a recipe, or bound to data in another view. Meaning appears at the surface but is not carried as a durable substrate. Fundamental's vocabulary is one answer to that failure, but the broader research question is whether interface systems should treat such meaning as a first-class layer.

---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [webcomponents]; [wcag-motion]; [use-of-color]; [chrome-devtools]; [fundamental-site].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is practical conformance: define automated tests proving that a canvas/WebGL/SVG expressive surface has not become the only carrier of meaning.

## 2. Background and related work

The semantic-canvas argument is grounded in platform and accessibility sources. HTML, DOM, and Web Components standards provide the semantic substrate and encapsulation model against which canvas/WebGL/SVG-only renderings should be compared [webcomponents]. WCAG motion and use-of-color criteria support the claim that expressive visual channels need redundant, accessible carriers when they communicate information or function [wcag-motion; use-of-color]. Browser DevTools anchors the practical claim that inspectable structure matters during authoring and debugging [chrome-devtools]. Fundamental's public documentation supplies the system example: the DOM is treated as the first semantic surface while expressive renderers remain secondary surfaces [fundamental-site].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Accessibility standards treat semantic availability and operability as non-negotiable constraints. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

## 3. Core model

The paper's model can be stated in four terms.

**Participant.** A participant is anything that can emit, receive, store, transform, or explain interface state. Participants include DOM elements, data records, relationships, layout regions, user attention, events, diagnostics, and visual layers. A rendered object is a participant only if it can be addressed by the model; visibility alone is not participation.

**Signal.** A signal is a typed property that can move between participants or be read from them. Examples include attention, memory, confidence, contradiction, priority, risk, uncertainty, relationship strength, and diagnostic cause. Signals may be visualized, but they should not be confused with the visualization.

**Binding.** A binding connects a participant to a source of meaning. A data record binds to a body; a source binds to a claim; a citation binds to an evidence relation; a motion trail binds to a semantic state; a diagnostic view binds to the runtime state it reveals. The binding determines whether the representation can be checked.

**Equivalence.** An equivalence is the rule by which a signal survives when a channel changes. Motion can become static emphasis; color can become text or shape; a canvas rendering can become semantic DOM; a particle path can become a relationship label. Equivalence is what prevents expressive interfaces from becoming inaccessible when their preferred representation is unavailable.

The model is falsifiable. If an interface shows a relation but the relation cannot be queried, it is not a participant. If a signal appears visually but has no semantic source, it is a decoration, not semantic state. If a reduced or alternate rendering drops meaning that the primary rendering carried, equivalence has failed. If diagnostics mutate the state they claim to explain, the diagnostic claim is invalid.

---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Name the semantic canvas problem: visible meaning without semantic participation.
- Define semantic source, visual binding, and fallback equivalence.
- Describe DOM-canonical architecture for expressive visuals.
- State conformance obligations for canvas/SVG/WebGL overlays.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

---

## 5. Design implications

The first implication is that interface behavior should be authored against explicit relations rather than against visual side effects. A designer should be able to name a support relation, a contradiction, a memory trace, an attention budget, or a priority field without smuggling that concept into an animation curve or color rule. This is the reason the recipe model matters: it separates product-language concepts from executable runtime tokens.

The second implication is that accessibility should be evaluated at the level of meaning. Turning off motion is not enough if motion was the only carrier of origin, direction, or causality. Removing color is not enough if color was the only carrier of confidence or risk. Replacing a canvas with fallback text is not enough if the relationships encoded by the canvas disappear. The substrate has to preserve the source meaning and expose an alternate carrier.

The third implication is that diagnostics should be part of the interface model rather than an afterthought. If the interface can compute a behavior, it should be able to expose why that behavior occurred. A runtime-inspectable interface does not require every user to inspect it, but it gives authors, auditors, and advanced users a path to check the system's claims.

The fourth implication is that data binding should preserve relational meaning. Rendering an array of records into cards loses the graph unless links, confidence, uncertainty, and lifecycle are also bound. A relational interface should not flatten data and then rebuild its meaning with badges.

---

## 6. Evaluation plan

A mature version of this paper would be evaluated through a family of studies rather than one omnibus experiment.

The first study would compare a conventional interface against a substrate-aware version on tasks that require orientation, refinding, and relationship awareness. The expected outcomes would include time to recover context, accuracy of relation identification, and subjective orientation.

The second study would test trust calibration. Participants would answer source-backed questions using either citations and badges or an explicit evidence graph. Outcomes would include discrimination between supported and unsupported claims, calibration error, and over-reliance under time pressure.

The third study would test semantic degradation. Participants would use a full expressive surface and a degraded surface with motion, color, or canvas effects removed. A non-inferiority design would ask whether comprehension and orientation are preserved when the expressive channel disappears.

The fourth study would test authoring and debugging. Authors would diagnose why a behavior occurred with and without runtime diagnostics. Outcomes would include time-to-diagnose, fix accuracy, and perceived confidence.

The studies should be preregistered and analyzed with mixed-effects models where tasks and participants are crossed, following the methods pattern used in the first family [baayen2008; nosek2018].

---

## 7. Implementation status and limitations

This draft should not be read as a claim that all proposed behavior is currently shipped. The first family already distinguishes shipped runtime features, study-page demonstrations, proposed harnesses, and unrun evaluations. The same discipline applies here. Fundamental provides a concrete implementation vocabulary — bodies, fields, recipes, registries, diagnostics, data binding, host boundaries — but this expansion paper argues the broader model.

The main limitation is abstraction risk. A general paper can become too broad to falsify. To avoid that, every concept above is tied to a failure condition: unqueryable relations, unsupported signals, inaccessible channel loss, mutating diagnostics, and flattened data. Future revisions should preserve those failure tests.

A second limitation is empirical status. The external bibliography now supports the paper's background and evaluation frame, but the paper's central proposal still requires the study or implementation evidence named in the research gap.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Canvas, WebGL, and high-fidelity visual layers often sever expressive behavior from semantic structure; platform-native expressive systems should keep semantic HTML canonical and bind advanced visuals to it as secondary representations. The claim is broader than Fundamental but made concrete by it. The first paper family shows the pieces: reading memory, evidence graphs, reduced-motion equivalence, host-driven runtime structure, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This expansion paper turns those pieces into a general research question: what would interface systems look like if relational meaning were treated as a substrate rather than an ornament?

========================================================================
FILE: docs/research/17-interaction-truth-modes.md
========================================================================

# Interaction Truth Modes: A Taxonomy for Honest Interface Behavior

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 17 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader taxonomy paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 17 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 01, Paper 03, Paper 06, Paper 08  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Interface behaviors need epistemic labels: authors and users should be able to tell whether a behavior is simulated, measured, inferred, expressive, diagnostic, decorative, or metaphorical. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — truth modes, epistemics, metaphor, interface behavior, and taxonomy — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: interface behaviors need epistemic labels: authors and users should be able to tell whether a behavior is simulated, measured, inferred, expressive, diagnostic, decorative, or metaphorical.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. They use motion, canvas, color, badges, animation, or layout to suggest a relation, but the relation itself is not available to the system. Fundamental's vocabulary is one answer to that failure, but the broader research question is whether interface systems should treat such meaning as a first-class layer.

---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [fruchterman1991]; [bostock2011]; [abdul2018]; [ragan2016]; [fundamental-engine-fieldtable].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is conceptual validation: the taxonomy should be tested by asking reviewers to classify real interface behaviors and identify overclaiming, ambiguity, or missing modes.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper proposes a taxonomy rather than claiming that an existing literature already uses these exact truth modes. Force-directed layout and D3 support the comparison class of physical or data-driven interface behavior whose visual effects can be mistaken for stronger claims than the system actually makes [fruchterman1991; bostock2011]. Explainability and provenance literature supports the need to expose the basis and limits of system behavior [abdul2018; ragan2016]. Fundamental's behavior table supplies the system-specific vocabulary for distinguishing its six truth modes — physical, designed, hybrid, diagnostic, poetic, and semantic [fundamental-engine-fieldtable].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Define physics truth, semantic truth, design truth, expressive truth, diagnostic truth, and metaphorical truth.
- Show how unlabeled metaphor creates false claims.
- Relate truth modes to passports, recipes, diagnostics, and accessibility equivalents.
- Propose authoring and review rules for honest behavior.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

---

# Field-Based Wayfinding for Long-Form Documents

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 18 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader empirical-hci paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 18 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 02, Paper 24, Paper 28  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Long-form documents should externalize where a reader is, where they have been, and which parts are related by turning sections, citations, cross-references, and dwell into a measured wayfinding field. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — document UX, wayfinding, TOC memory maps, citation paths, and orientation — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. ## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [mangen2013]; [ackerman2011]; [clinton2019]; [conklin1987]; [furnas1986]; [pirolli1999].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is direct evaluation of field-based wayfinding features: memory-weighted tables of contents, citation return paths, section trails, and relation-aware refinding.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper is grounded in reading, hypertext, and information-foraging literature. Screen-vs-paper reading studies support the narrower premise that screen reading can differ from paper reading in orientation, metacognition, and comprehension conditions, especially when spatial/kinesthetic cues are weaker [mangen2013; ackerman2011; clinton2019]. Hypertext and fisheye/overview work support the wayfinding frame: readers need to know where they are, where they can go, and how local content relates to surrounding structure [conklin1987; furnas1986]. Information-foraging theory supports the idea that navigational cues can act as scent and that previously traversed terrain can be made more legible [pirolli1999].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. The paper's model can be stated in four terms.

A rendered object is a participant only if it can be addressed by the model; visibility alone is not participation.

**Signal.** A signal is a typed property that can move between participants or be read from them. Examples include attention, memory, confidence, contradiction, priority, risk, uncertainty, relationship strength, and diagnostic cause. **Binding.** A binding connects a participant to a source of meaning. ---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Narrow the Reading Field to wayfinding rather than all reading cognition.
- Define current-position, memory-map, citation-return, and relationship-trail surfaces.
- Propose tasks for refinding, orientation recovery, and relationship awareness.
- Make reduced-motion and semantic baseline conditions explicit.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

If the interface can compute a behavior, it should be able to expose why that behavior occurred. A runtime-inspectable interface does not require every user to inspect it, but it gives authors, auditors, and advanced users a path to check the system's claims.

The fourth implication is that data binding should preserve relational meaning. Rendering an array of records into cards loses the graph unless links, confidence, uncertainty, and lifecycle are also bound. A relational interface should not flatten data and then rebuild its meaning with badges.

---

## 6. Evaluation plan

A mature version of this paper would be evaluated through a family of studies rather than one omnibus experiment.

The first study would compare a conventional interface against a substrate-aware version on tasks that require orientation, refinding, and relationship awareness. The expected outcomes would include time to recover context, accuracy of relation identification, and subjective orientation.

The second study would test trust calibration. Participants would answer source-backed questions using either citations and badges or an explicit evidence graph. Outcomes would include discrimination between supported and unsupported claims, calibration error, and over-reliance under time pressure.

The third study would test semantic degradation. Participants would use a full expressive surface and a degraded surface with motion, color, or canvas effects removed. A non-inferiority design would ask whether comprehension and orientation are preserved when the expressive channel disappears.

The fourth study would test authoring and debugging. Authors would diagnose why a behavior occurred with and without runtime diagnostics. Outcomes would include time-to-diagnose, fix accuracy, and perceived confidence.

The studies should be preregistered and analyzed with mixed-effects models where tasks and participants are crossed, following the methods pattern used in the first family [baayen2008; nosek2018].

---

## 7. Implementation status and limitations

This draft should not be read as a claim that all proposed behavior is currently shipped. The first family already distinguishes shipped runtime features, study-page demonstrations, proposed harnesses, and unrun evaluations. The same discipline applies here. Fundamental provides a concrete implementation vocabulary — bodies, fields, recipes, registries, diagnostics, data binding, host boundaries — but this expansion paper argues the broader model.

The main limitation is abstraction risk. A general paper can become too broad to falsify. To avoid that, every concept above is tied to a failure condition: unqueryable relations, unsupported signals, inaccessible channel loss, mutating diagnostics, and flattened data. Future revisions should preserve those failure tests.

A second limitation is empirical status. The external bibliography now supports the paper's background and evaluation frame, but the paper's central proposal still requires the study or implementation evidence named in the research gap.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Long-form documents should externalize where a reader is, where they have been, and which parts are related by turning sections, citations, cross-references, and dwell into a measured wayfinding field. The claim is broader than Fundamental but made concrete by it. The first paper family shows the pieces: reading memory, evidence graphs, reduced-motion equivalence, host-driven runtime structure, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This expansion paper turns those pieces into a general research question: what would interface systems look like if relational meaning were treated as a substrate rather than an ornament?

========================================================================
FILE: docs/research/19-contradiction-aware-interfaces.md
========================================================================

# Contradiction-Aware Interfaces

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 19 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader ai-hci paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 19 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 03, Paper 12, Paper 29  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Interfaces should treat contradiction as a first-class state rather than an absence of agreement: opposing evidence, conflicting data, and inconsistent recommendations need visible structure, not silent flattening into a single answer. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — contradiction, evidence conflict, uncertainty, decision support, and AI interfaces — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: interfaces should treat contradiction as a first-class state rather than an absence of agreement: opposing evidence, conflicting data, and inconsistent recommendations need visible structure, not silent flattening into a single answer.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. A system becomes interesting when relationships, evidence, attention, memory, diagnostics, accessibility equivalents, and data lifecycle stop being scattered across component props and one-off event handlers and become inspectable participants in a shared model.

This distinction matters because many expressive interfaces fail in the same pattern. They use motion, canvas, color, badges, animation, or layout to suggest a relation, but the relation itself is not available to the system. It cannot be inspected, tested, degraded for accessibility, serialized as a recipe, or bound to data in another view. Meaning appears at the surface but is not carried as a durable substrate. Fundamental's vocabulary is one answer to that failure, but the broader research question is whether interface systems should treat such meaning as a first-class layer.

---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [lee2004]; [hullman2019]; [liu2023verifiability]; [wallat2024faithfulness]; [bucinca2021]; [kim2025llm].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is to test contradiction-specific displays independently from broader evidence graphs so the effect of surfacing conflict can be measured cleanly.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper uses trust calibration, uncertainty visualization, and RAG-attribution work to isolate contradiction as an interface problem. Trust-calibration work supports the goal of appropriate reliance rather than persuasion [lee2004]. Uncertainty-visualization work supports the caution that visual encodings can change how uncertainty is perceived and acted on [hullman2019]. Generative-search and attribution-faithfulness work supports the premise that source-backed output may contain unsupported, weakly supported, or unfaithfully attributed claims [liu2023verifiability; wallat2024faithfulness]. Cognitive-forcing and LLM reliance studies support the evaluation concern that inconsistency cues may reduce overreliance only if they are legible and task-relevant [bucinca2021; kim2025llm].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Accessibility standards treat semantic availability and operability as non-negotiable constraints. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

## 3. Core model

The paper's model can be stated in four terms.

**Participant.** A participant is anything that can emit, receive, store, transform, or explain interface state. Participants include DOM elements, data records, relationships, layout regions, user attention, events, diagnostics, and visual layers. A rendered object is a participant only if it can be addressed by the model; visibility alone is not participation.

**Signal.** A signal is a typed property that can move between participants or be read from them. Examples include attention, memory, confidence, contradiction, priority, risk, uncertainty, relationship strength, and diagnostic cause. Signals may be visualized, but they should not be confused with the visualization.

**Binding.** A binding connects a participant to a source of meaning. A data record binds to a body; a source binds to a claim; a citation binds to an evidence relation; a motion trail binds to a semantic state; a diagnostic view binds to the runtime state it reveals. The binding determines whether the representation can be checked.

**Equivalence.** An equivalence is the rule by which a signal survives when a channel changes. Motion can become static emphasis; color can become text or shape; a canvas rendering can become semantic DOM; a particle path can become a relationship label. Equivalence is what prevents expressive interfaces from becoming inaccessible when their preferred representation is unavailable.

The model is falsifiable. If an interface shows a relation but the relation cannot be queried, it is not a participant. If a signal appears visually but has no semantic source, it is a decoration, not semantic state. If a reduced or alternate rendering drops meaning that the primary rendering carried, equivalence has failed. If diagnostics mutate the state they claim to explain, the diagnostic claim is invalid.

---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Define contradiction as a typed relationship, not a sentiment.
- Distinguish disagreement, contradiction, ambiguity, uncertainty, and absence of support.
- Describe visual and semantic encodings for conflict.
- Propose calibration and decision-quality measures.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

---

## 5. Design implications

The first implication is that interface behavior should be authored against explicit relations rather than against visual side effects. A designer should be able to name a support relation, a contradiction, a memory trace, an attention budget, or a priority field without smuggling that concept into an animation curve or color rule. This is the reason the recipe model matters: it separates product-language concepts from executable runtime tokens.

The second implication is that accessibility should be evaluated at the level of meaning. Turning off motion is not enough if motion was the only carrier of origin, direction, or causality. Removing color is not enough if color was the only carrier of confidence or risk. Replacing a canvas with fallback text is not enough if the relationships encoded by the canvas disappear. The substrate has to preserve the source meaning and expose an alternate carrier.

The third implication is that diagnostics should be part of the interface model rather than an afterthought. If the interface can compute a behavior, it should be able to expose why that behavior occurred. A runtime-inspectable interface does not require every user to inspect it, but it gives authors, auditors, and advanced users a path to check the system's claims.

The fourth implication is that data binding should preserve relational meaning. Rendering an array of records into cards loses the graph unless links, confidence, uncertainty, and lifecycle are also bound. A relational interface should not flatten data and then rebuild its meaning with badges.

---

## 6. Evaluation plan

A mature version of this paper would be evaluated through a family of studies rather than one omnibus experiment.

The first study would compare a conventional interface against a substrate-aware version on tasks that require orientation, refinding, and relationship awareness. The expected outcomes would include time to recover context, accuracy of relation identification, and subjective orientation.

The second study would test trust calibration. Participants would answer source-backed questions using either citations and badges or an explicit evidence graph. Outcomes would include discrimination between supported and unsupported claims, calibration error, and over-reliance under time pressure.

The third study would test semantic degradation. Participants would use a full expressive surface and a degraded surface with motion, color, or canvas effects removed. A non-inferiority design would ask whether comprehension and orientation are preserved when the expressive channel disappears.

The fourth study would test authoring and debugging. Authors would diagnose why a behavior occurred with and without runtime diagnostics. Outcomes would include time-to-diagnose, fix accuracy, and perceived confidence.

The studies should be preregistered and analyzed with mixed-effects models where tasks and participants are crossed, following the methods pattern used in the first family [baayen2008; nosek2018].

---

## 7. Implementation status and limitations

This draft should not be read as a claim that all proposed behavior is currently shipped. The first family already distinguishes shipped runtime features, study-page demonstrations, proposed harnesses, and unrun evaluations. The same discipline applies here. Fundamental provides a concrete implementation vocabulary — bodies, fields, recipes, registries, diagnostics, data binding, host boundaries — but this expansion paper argues the broader model.

The main limitation is abstraction risk. A general paper can become too broad to falsify. To avoid that, every concept above is tied to a failure condition: unqueryable relations, unsupported signals, inaccessible channel loss, mutating diagnostics, and flattened data. Future revisions should preserve those failure tests.

A second limitation is empirical status. The external bibliography now supports the paper's background and evaluation frame, but the paper's central proposal still requires the study or implementation evidence named in the research gap.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Interfaces should treat contradiction as a first-class state rather than an absence of agreement: opposing evidence, conflicting data, and inconsistent recommendations need visible structure, not silent flattening into a single answer. The claim is broader than Fundamental but made concrete by it. The first paper family shows the pieces: reading memory, evidence graphs, reduced-motion equivalence, host-driven runtime structure, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This expansion paper turns those pieces into a general research question: what would interface systems look like if relational meaning were treated as a substrate rather than an ornament?

FILE: docs/research/20-non-perturbing-diagnostics.md

# Non-Perturbing Diagnostics for Interactive Systems

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 20 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader systems paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 20 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 08, Paper 13  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

A diagnostic interface should reveal the behavior it observes without becoming part of the behavior it measures; diagnostic layers must be read-only, separable, and auditable. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — diagnostics, observability, read-only inspection, debugging, and simulation — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. ---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [chrome-devtools]; [firefox-devtools]; [meszaros2007]; [fastdom]; [webdev-layout-thrashing].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is to formalize and test non-perturbation for richer diagnostic overlays, especially when diagnostics add visual load or event handling of their own.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper's core invariant is systems-oriented. Browser DevTools provide the practical lineage for inspecting running interfaces without treating the inspector itself as the primary behavior [chrome-devtools; firefox-devtools]. Test-pattern literature supports the separation between test harnesses and the system under test [meszaros2007]. DOM read/write batching and layout-thrashing guidance support the concrete web-performance version of the same principle: reads and writes must be disciplined because observing or measuring at the wrong time can perturb the behavior being measured [fastdom; webdev-layout-thrashing].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. ---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Define non-perturbation for interactive systems.
- Separate diagnostic sampling from control feedback.
- Identify risks: measurement writes, layout reads during mutation, inspector state leaking into runtime state.
- Propose conformance checks for diagnostic purity.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

# Host-Driven UI Runtimes: Portability Through Environmental Injection

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 21 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader systems paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 21 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 05, Paper 30  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Portable UI behavior runtimes should isolate environment contact behind explicit host interfaces, so the behavior core can run across browser, headless, worker, native, and alternative render targets without reaching for globals. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — host boundary, ports and adapters, headless UI, renderer portability, and testability — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. ## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [cockburn2005]; [headless-ui]; [react-test-renderer]; [retained-immediate-ui]; [meszaros2007].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is engineering validation across more targets: browser DOM, headless tests, workers, native surfaces, and alternative renderers should all exercise the same core behavior through host injection.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper generalizes the host boundary as an architectural pattern. Ports-and-adapters/hexagonal architecture supports the separation of a domain core from environment-specific adapters [cockburn2005]. Headless UI and React test-renderer documentation support the comparison cases: behavior can be separated from a particular visual implementation or renderer, but those patterns do not by themselves define a host boundary for every environmental dependency [headless-ui; react-test-renderer]. Retained/immediate-mode UI literature and test-pattern work support the broader systems framing of render-target separation and testability [retained-immediate-ui; meszaros2007].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Accessibility standards treat semantic availability and operability as non-negotiable constraints. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

## 3. Core model

The paper's model can be stated in four terms.

**Participant.** A participant is anything that can emit, receive, store, transform, or explain interface state. Participants include DOM elements, data records, relationships, layout regions, user attention, events, diagnostics, and visual layers. A rendered object is a participant only if it can be addressed by the model; visibility alone is not participation.

**Signal.** A signal is a typed property that can move between participants or be read from them. Examples include attention, memory, confidence, contradiction, priority, risk, uncertainty, relationship strength, and diagnostic cause. Signals may be visualized, but they should not be confused with the visualization.

**Binding.** A binding connects a participant to a source of meaning. A data record binds to a body; a source binds to a claim; a citation binds to an evidence relation; a motion trail binds to a semantic state; a diagnostic view binds to the runtime state it reveals. The binding determines whether the representation can be checked.

**Equivalence.** An equivalence is the rule by which a signal survives when a channel changes. ---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Generalize FieldHost into a host-driven runtime pattern.
- Define host responsibilities: time, viewport, input, visibility, rendering factories, environment preferences.
- Show testability and portability as consequences of the same seam.
- State failure modes: hidden globals, implicit scheduler ownership, backend-specific assumptions.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

---

## 5. Design implications

The first implication is that interface behavior should be authored against explicit relations rather than against visual side effects. A designer should be able to name a support relation, a contradiction, a memory trace, an attention budget, or a priority field without smuggling that concept into an animation curve or color rule. This is the reason the recipe model matters: it separates product-language concepts from executable runtime tokens.

The second implication is that accessibility should be evaluated at the level of meaning. Turning off motion is not enough if motion was the only carrier of origin, direction, or causality. Removing color is not enough if color was the only carrier of confidence or risk. Replacing a canvas with fallback text is not enough if the relationships encoded by the canvas disappear. The substrate has to preserve the source meaning and expose an alternate carrier.

The third implication is that diagnostics should be part of the interface model rather than an afterthought. If the interface can compute a behavior, it should be able to expose why that behavior occurred. A runtime-inspectable interface does not require every user to inspect it, but it gives authors, auditors, and advanced users a path to check the system's claims.

The fourth implication is that data binding should preserve relational meaning. Rendering an array of records into cards loses the graph unless links, confidence, uncertainty, and lifecycle are also bound. A relational interface should not flatten data and then rebuild its meaning with badges.

---

## 6. Evaluation plan

A mature version of this paper would be evaluated through a family of studies rather than one omnibus experiment.

The first study would compare a conventional interface against a substrate-aware version on tasks that require orientation, refinding, and relationship awareness. The expected outcomes would include time to recover context, accuracy of relation identification, and subjective orientation.

The second study would test trust calibration. Participants would answer source-backed questions using either citations and badges or an explicit evidence graph. Outcomes would include discrimination between supported and unsupported claims, calibration error, and over-reliance under time pressure.

The third study would test semantic degradation. Participants would use a full expressive surface and a degraded surface with motion, color, or canvas effects removed. A non-inferiority design would ask whether comprehension and orientation are preserved when the expressive channel disappears.

The fourth study would test authoring and debugging. Authors would diagnose why a behavior occurred with and without runtime diagnostics. Outcomes would include time-to-diagnose, fix accuracy, and perceived confidence.

The studies should be preregistered and analyzed with mixed-effects models where tasks and participants are crossed, following the methods pattern used in the first family [baayen2008; nosek2018].

---

## 7. Implementation status and limitations

This draft should not be read as a claim that all proposed behavior is currently shipped. The first family already distinguishes shipped runtime features, study-page demonstrations, proposed harnesses, and unrun evaluations. The same discipline applies here. Fundamental provides a concrete implementation vocabulary — bodies, fields, recipes, registries, diagnostics, data binding, host boundaries — but this expansion paper argues the broader model.

The main limitation is abstraction risk. A general paper can become too broad to falsify. To avoid that, every concept above is tied to a failure condition: unqueryable relations, unsupported signals, inaccessible channel loss, mutating diagnostics, and flattened data. Future revisions should preserve those failure tests.

A second limitation is empirical status. The external bibliography now supports the paper's background and evaluation frame, but the paper's central proposal still requires the study or implementation evidence named in the research gap.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Portable UI behavior runtimes should isolate environment contact behind explicit host interfaces, so the behavior core can run across browser, headless, worker, native, and alternative render targets without reaching for globals. The claim is broader than Fundamental but made concrete by it. The first paper family shows the pieces: reading memory, evidence graphs, reduced-motion equivalence, host-driven runtime structure, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This expansion paper turns those pieces into a general research question: what would interface systems look like if relational meaning were treated as a substrate rather than an ornament?

========================================================================
FILE: docs/research/22-accessibility-conformance-expressive-interfaces.md
========================================================================

# Accessibility Conformance for Expressive Interfaces

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 22 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader accessibility paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 22 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 04, Paper 11, Paper 16, Paper 25  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Expressive interface systems need conformance machinery, not only accessibility intent: schemas, lint rules, previews, source-of-meaning traces, and equivalence checks should make accessibility claims testable. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — accessibility conformance, lint, schemas, reduced mode, and semantic preservation — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: expressive interface systems need conformance machinery, not only accessibility intent: schemas, lint rules, previews, source-of-meaning traces, and equivalence checks should make accessibility claims testable.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. A system becomes interesting when relationships, evidence, attention, memory, diagnostics, accessibility equivalents, and data lifecycle stop being scattered across component props and one-off event handlers and become inspectable participants in a shared model.

This distinction matters because many expressive interfaces fail in the same pattern. They use motion, canvas, color, badges, animation, or layout to suggest a relation, but the relation itself is not available to the system. It cannot be inspected, tested, degraded for accessibility, serialized as a recipe, or bound to data in another view. Meaning appears at the surface but is not carried as a durable substrate. Fundamental's vocabulary is one answer to that failure, but the broader research question is whether interface systems should treat such meaning as a first-class layer.

---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [wcag-motion]; [use-of-color]; [prefers-reduced-motion]; [vestibular-accessibility]; [piaggio2012].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is conformance automation: how much semantic equivalence can be checked by lint/schema/runtime preview, and where human review remains necessary.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper differs from Paper 11 by focusing on conformance machinery rather than the general theory of degradation. WCAG motion and use-of-color criteria provide the normative requirements for controllable motion and redundant meaning carriers [wcag-motion; use-of-color]. Media Queries Level 5 supplies the platform preference signal for reduced motion [prefers-reduced-motion]. WAI motion guidance supports a cautious user-impact framing without requiring unsupported prevalence claims [vestibular-accessibility]. Non-inferiority guidance supports the proposed study design for showing that reduced or alternate surfaces are not materially worse on meaning-recovery tasks [piaggio2012].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Accessibility standards treat semantic availability and operability as non-negotiable constraints. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

## 3. Core model

The paper's model can be stated in four terms.

**Participant.** A participant is anything that can emit, receive, store, transform, or explain interface state. Participants include DOM elements, data records, relationships, layout regions, user attention, events, diagnostics, and visual layers. A rendered object is a participant only if it can be addressed by the model; visibility alone is not participation.

**Signal.** A signal is a typed property that can move between participants or be read from them. Examples include attention, memory, confidence, contradiction, priority, risk, uncertainty, relationship strength, and diagnostic cause. Signals may be visualized, but they should not be confused with the visualization.

**Binding.** A binding connects a participant to a source of meaning. A data record binds to a body; a source binds to a claim; a citation binds to an evidence relation; a motion trail binds to a semantic state; a diagnostic view binds to the runtime state it reveals. The binding determines whether the representation can be checked.

**Equivalence.** An equivalence is the rule by which a signal survives when a channel changes. Motion can become static emphasis; color can become text or shape; a canvas rendering can become semantic DOM; a particle path can become a relationship label. Equivalence is what prevents expressive interfaces from becoming inaccessible when their preferred representation is unavailable.

The model is falsifiable. If an interface shows a relation but the relation cannot be queried, it is not a participant. If a signal appears visually but has no semantic source, it is a decoration, not semantic state. If a reduced or alternate rendering drops meaning that the primary rendering carried, equivalence has failed. If diagnostics mutate the state they claim to explain, the diagnostic claim is invalid.

---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Define expressive accessibility as a testable contract.
- Generalize reduced-motion conformance to channel conformance.
- Specify gates: semantic source, redundant carrier, reduced-mode equivalent, preview, lint, study protocol.
- Distinguish WCAG compliance from stronger meaning-equivalence claims.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

---

## 5. Design implications

The first implication is that interface behavior should be authored against explicit relations rather than against visual side effects. A designer should be able to name a support relation, a contradiction, a memory trace, an attention budget, or a priority field without smuggling that concept into an animation curve or color rule. This is the reason the recipe model matters: it separates product-language concepts from executable runtime tokens.

The second implication is that accessibility should be evaluated at the level of meaning. Turning off motion is not enough if motion was the only carrier of origin, direction, or causality. Removing color is not enough if color was the only carrier of confidence or risk. Replacing a canvas with fallback text is not enough if the relationships encoded by the canvas disappear. The substrate has to preserve the source meaning and expose an alternate carrier.

The third implication is that diagnostics should be part of the interface model rather than an afterthought. If the interface can compute a behavior, it should be able to expose why that behavior occurred. A runtime-inspectable interface does not require every user to inspect it, but it gives authors, auditors, and advanced users a path to check the system's claims.

The fourth implication is that data binding should preserve relational meaning. Rendering an array of records into cards loses the graph unless links, confidence, uncertainty, and lifecycle are also bound. A relational interface should not flatten data and then rebuild its meaning with badges.

---

## 6. Evaluation plan

A mature version of this paper would be evaluated through a family of studies rather than one omnibus experiment.

The first study would compare a conventional interface against a substrate-aware version on tasks that require orientation, refinding, and relationship awareness. The expected outcomes would include time to recover context, accuracy of relation identification, and subjective orientation.

The second study would test trust calibration. Participants would answer source-backed questions using either citations and badges or an explicit evidence graph. Outcomes would include discrimination between supported and unsupported claims, calibration error, and over-reliance under time pressure.

The third study would test semantic degradation. Participants would use a full expressive surface and a degraded surface with motion, color, or canvas effects removed. A non-inferiority design would ask whether comprehension and orientation are preserved when the expressive channel disappears.

The fourth study would test authoring and debugging. Authors would diagnose why a behavior occurred with and without runtime diagnostics. Outcomes would include time-to-diagnose, fix accuracy, and perceived confidence.

The studies should be preregistered and analyzed with mixed-effects models where tasks and participants are crossed, following the methods pattern used in the first family [baayen2008; nosek2018].

---

## 7. Implementation status and limitations

This draft should not be read as a claim that all proposed behavior is currently shipped. The first family already distinguishes shipped runtime features, study-page demonstrations, proposed harnesses, and unrun evaluations. The same discipline applies here. Fundamental provides a concrete implementation vocabulary — bodies, fields, recipes, registries, diagnostics, data binding, host boundaries — but this expansion paper argues the broader model.

The main limitation is abstraction risk. A general paper can become too broad to falsify. To avoid that, every concept above is tied to a failure condition: unqueryable relations, unsupported signals, inaccessible channel loss, mutating diagnostics, and flattened data. Future revisions should preserve those failure tests.

A second limitation is empirical status. The external bibliography now supports the paper's background and evaluation frame, but the paper's central proposal still requires the study or implementation evidence named in the research gap.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Expressive interface systems need conformance machinery, not only accessibility intent: schemas, lint rules, previews, source-of-meaning traces, and equivalence checks should make accessibility claims testable. The claim is broader than Fundamental but made concrete by it. The first paper family shows the pieces: reading memory, evidence graphs, reduced-motion equivalence, host-driven runtime structure, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This expansion paper turns those pieces into a general research question: what would interface systems look like if relational meaning were treated as a substrate rather than an ornament?

# Relationship Registries as an Interface Primitive

> **Status: citation-complete expansion draft (editorial review copy).** Paper 23 of the Fundamental expansion family. This paper generalizes claims from the first eight Fundamental papers into a broader systems-hci paper. It is not yet externally peer reviewed, and any empirical section below is a study design rather than a result.

**Author:** Zach Shallbetter  
**Series:** Fundamental Research Expansion Papers, Paper 23 of 30  
**Depends on:** Paper 02, Paper 03, Paper 07, Paper 08, Paper 28  
**Primary source family:** Papers 01–08 and the Fundamental Engine documentation [fundamental-site; fundamental-engine-repo]

---

## Abstract

Relationships between interface elements should be explicit runtime objects, not incidental layout, prose, hidden application state, or ARIA hints alone. This paper isolates that claim from the implementation-specific language of the first Fundamental paper family and states it as a portable interface principle. The contribution is threefold. First, it defines the core concept in operational terms: what state must exist, where that state lives, who can read it, and what makes the claim falsifiable. Second, it positions the concept against adjacent interface traditions — relationships, registries, interface topology, DOM semantics, and typed edges — so the difference is not merely terminological. Third, it sketches a concrete evaluation and conformance plan that can be run without assuming that Fundamental is the only possible implementation. The paper is therefore a bridge: grounded in Fundamental's shipped vocabulary and documentation, but written so the model can be argued, criticized, and reproduced outside that codebase.

---

## 1. Introduction

Modern interfaces routinely contain more meaning than their rendered components expose. A result is not only a card; it has relevance, source support, uncertainty, recency, and relation to neighboring results. A document section is not only a block of text; it has position, prior attention, cross-references, and a role in the reader's mental map. A dashboard tile is not only a rectangle; it participates in a larger state of risk, priority, anomaly, and dependency. Conventional UI systems can render these properties, but they rarely make them first-class interface state.

The first Fundamental paper family approaches this problem through a relational field runtime. The flagship paper names the general model; the supporting papers test that model against reading, evidence, motion/accessibility, host portability, recipes, data binding, and diagnostics. This paper pulls one reusable claim out of that family: relationships between interface elements should be explicit runtime objects, not incidental layout, prose, hidden application state, or aria hints alone.

The point is not to argue that every interface should contain particles, simulated forces, or visual field lines. Those are representations. The deeper claim is about interface state. ---


## Citation status and research gap

**Verified source role.** This paper's load-bearing background claims are keyed to: [webcomponents]; [conklin1987]; [nielsen1990hypertext]; [bostock2011]; [ragan2016].

**Remaining research gap.** The remaining research gap is to show that first-class typed relationship registries improve authoring, auditing, accessibility, or user task performance over implicit links and ad hoc application state.

## 2. Background and related work

This paper positions explicit relationship registries between platform structure and visualization. Web Components and DOM standards support the claim that interface structure can be componentized and encapsulated, but not that semantic relationships across components are automatically typed and queryable [webcomponents]. Hypertext literature supports the long-standing importance of links and navigable relationships [conklin1987; nielsen1990hypertext]. D3 and provenance literature support the adjacent idea that relationships can drive visual structure and that the basis of those relationships should remain inspectable [bostock2011; ragan2016].

The adjacent systems differ by what they treat as primary. Component systems treat local component state as the organizing unit. Reactive renderers treat a view as a function of application state. Scene graphs treat drawable objects and their transform hierarchy as the durable model. Information visualization treats data structure and visual encoding as primary. Accessibility standards treat semantic availability and operability as non-negotiable constraints. Fundamental's field model intersects all of these but is not reducible to any one of them: it treats rendered elements, data records, relationships, metrics, accessibility equivalents, and diagnostics as participants in a shared interface substrate.

The distinction is important for this paper's scope. A semantic substrate is not merely a data model behind a view. It is also not merely a visualization of application state. It is the layer that determines what the interface can honestly say about itself: what exists, what relates, what changed, what should remain available when a visual channel disappears, and what can be inspected when behavior becomes confusing.

---

## 4. Contributions

This paper contributes the following claims.

- Define relationship registries as typed, inspectable edge stores.
- Explain why links, layout adjacency, ARIA references, and application objects are insufficient alone.
- Show four domains: documents, evidence, data dashboards, diagnostics.
- Propose authoring, inspection, and accessibility obligations.

Together these claims make the concept stronger than a metaphor. They state what must be implemented, what must be inspectable, and what can fail.

---
